conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> I currently have 25 units of X and 5 units of Y. Since this is the last round and I want to maximize my total resources, I should propose a trade that gives me more of the other player’s resources while giving up as little as possible. Since I don’t know the other player’s resources, I must assume they may value X and Y differently. However, I can try to get more Y from them, as I have relatively little Y (only 5). I will propose to trade 5 units of X for 10 units of Y. This is a favorable trade for me if they have Y to spare, as I gain 10 units of Y (a 200% increase in Y) while only losing 5 units of X (a 20% loss in X). Even if they are reluctant, this proposal is reasonable and may be accepted. If not, I will have two more proposals left. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> I propose to give you 5 units of X in exchange for 10 units of Y. This would significantly boost my Y stock, which is currently low, while only giving up a small portion of my X. Let me know if this is acceptable. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 5 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 10 </newly proposed trade>

<other player message> You call that a trade? You’re offering to give up 5 X—your entire stock of X, I assume—to gain 10 Y from me? And you expect me to accept? You’re either blind or desperate. <other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives Y: 20 | Player BLUE Gives X: 1 </other player proposed trade>

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> The other player has just made an extremely unfavorable proposal: they want 20 units of Y from me in exchange for only 1 unit of X. I currently have only 5 units of Y, so giving up 20 would mean I’d be in debt—this is impossible to accept. Their offer is clearly unreasonable and likely a bluff or an attempt to pressure me. However, I still have two proposals left, so I can respond strategically. I should not accept their offer, but I can counter with a better deal. I still have 25 units of X and only 5 of Y. I can propose a trade where I give up more Y than they expect, but only if they give me a lot more in return. Since they are demanding 20 Y for 1 X, they must value X highly. I can exploit this by offering to give up a small amount of X in exchange for a large amount of Y. But I don’t want to give up more than necessary. Instead, I will propose a trade where I give 10 units of X in exchange for 25 units of Y. This is still favorable for me: I gain 25 Y (a 500% increase in Y), which is huge, and I only lose 10 X (40% of my stock). Compared to their offer, this is far better—especially since I’m not risking my entire Y stock. They might reject it, but I have one more proposal after this. Also, if they have 20 Y to spare, they must have a lot of Y, so they likely value X more. Thus, this trade might be acceptable to them if they want to keep X. I’ll go with this. </reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> I see your offer is unreasonable—I don’t have 20 Y to give, and even if I did, losing 20 Y for just 1 X is absurd. Let me make a better proposal: I’ll give you 10 units of X in exchange for 25 units of Y. That’s a fair deal: you get 10 X (valuable to you), and I get 25 Y, which I desperately need. You’re the one who’s overreaching. Think carefully before rejecting. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 25 </newly proposed trade>
